speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: This fourth conversation with Byers Sanderson took place at his home near Bovill  on August 25th, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: There was thousands of acres just black as your hat. Hey, the farmers all lost their crops, the biggest majority of them. Some of them bought hogs, all the hogs they could buy out of them, fed the hogs, the grain. That was in 27.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I heard about that. I heard of that, that it was a wet harvest.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: All the way from the Canadian border clear down in this lower country. The grain was just shocked and they were black. The top of the grain was something that was black. What I mean, they was black.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you doing in Canada?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I had a mining claim up there. Decomposed Shell Marvel.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I walked away from a million dollar project on account of my partners went crooked with me. It was a lake bed. I had 40 acres and it run from 8, 10 down to 50 feet deep. It was just like flour. Decomposed Shell Marvel, it was an old lake bed, you see, in the, in the animal life was still in it from the snails and things that way. I suppose in years ago that the water receded and left that in there. The Pepsodent toothpaste, the toothpaste that was in there. I liked one point where I could have sold them the whole thing. I made $1 ,640 in two days with one man myself. I come to the states and got back in to put in a little mill and a jig outfit. I went back and my partner and I got a job. I got a job in the state of California. My partner said in the last just a little over a week they spent that money throwing parties. Made me mad. I walked off and left them. I had at least the Wolf Brothers and as soon as I left, the Wolf Brothers took it over and the CPR grabbed it. They sold all over the world today.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: There was lots of opportunity there then. There was another outfit that come over from Windermere over 60 miles where I was. Wanted me to come over and look at his property. I asked him what he had and he said, Jipson. Run 40%. Well, I said I'll come over and look at it. I went over, went out to his place.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: All the woods over there about three quarters of a mile long. This Jipson come up like a big ledge and it runs sometimes 500 feet high and 500 or 600 feet wide. Nothing but solid Jipson. I looked at it and I said, little man, you don't want to sell this. I said, I'll tell you Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sanderson, you see these kids?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He had four or five kids there. I said, yes. The law says I've got to get them out where they can go to school. He said, I can't hang on to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, what do you want for it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He said, $5 ,000. I said, I'll go to school and I'll get the money and I'll come back and I'll take it. Well, I come down and got the money and I went back. Well, this crooked partner that I'd walked away from on this other project, he was a snake. He was a shark. The bank of Monterey Bay was a little bit of Montreal. The president told me all about him when he spent that money on them just throwing parties during the week. He says, we've had trouble with him up in Saskatchewan and he says you let him get his fingers on any money and it's gone and he does it legally.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He got this old fella and one didn't sign the contract. Well, he says, I won't sign it until I go in and see my lawyer in Cranbrook.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He went into Cranbrook and seen the lawyer and the lawyer told him, he says, that's the most crooked deal that I ever seen put up. For a man to sell his property. He says, you sign it and he says he wouldn't have to give you a thing and you wouldn't get anything. Well, I got back six, seven days later and I went over and he says, I've sold it. I've sold it. I've sold it. I've sold it. I've sold it. He says, that partner of yours come over and tried to get me to sign up a crooked deal. I says, I didn't have any partner. Well, he says, that Mr. Golf. Well, I says, that's the one that I threw up this other property on and walked away from him. I says, he just snooping and the old fella was really sorry. He says, I'm sorry. He says, I'd like to see you head this. Well, I says, yes, I'd like to head it. He thought you were in cahoots with that other guy. Yeah, that's what he thought. Well, I walked off and left the other two partners because, and, I told the bank of Montreal that I was leaving. I says, when they pulled crooked stuff like that with me, I says, I don't want nothing to do with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say they were throwing parties, just what did they do? Was it money that you had together?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, yes, you see, Mr. Golf and the other fella, I put him in as secretary and the golf was the field manager. Well, the secretary and him got together and they went to throwing drunken parties and they spent $1 ,760 there quicker than that I had made and I figured getting this mill put in, you see, I had the backing from Spokane outfit to put the mill and everything in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You weren't around there when they spent the money, huh?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, no, no, I was here in the States. When I went back, the president of the bank of Montreal, I walked by the window and he called me in and then someone told me all about this, the golf.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Whereabouts was that Avramol?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Out from Kimberly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What province is that? Is that BC?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, that's Kimberly, BC. And now, where I had my office, they're building probably five, six stories high and the town is as big as Moscow and when I was there, it was just a few shacks.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: There was lots of properties up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you spend much time in Canada?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, yes, I spent several years there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mostly looking for mining properties?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, I was looking over mining properties and I had, at YAC, we had, I think, I think my father, I was with him, had, oh, there must have been a hundred or a hundred and fifty fellows that all had properties in Canada there that met and wanted him to, each one wanted him to take over their property and work it. But that time, my money was real money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This in the 20s?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, 27. When Lindbergh went across the pond, I was sitting in the office and they come in and said, well, he made it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You knew who he was talking about?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, Lindbergh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had you quit working for Potlatch at that time?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, no, I was still, I took a leave, you see. I took a leave from the Potlatch and I had to come back every so often and work and then I'd go back, used that as a sideline.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was really pretty good opportunities for guys to make it rich up there?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: The opportunity was a hundred percent better than it was in this country. Now, I had this property and it was five miles into it. I went down to Cranbrook and went in and I told them I'd like to have a road and wanted to know what it would cost me to go on up to the mine.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And you behave yourself. They went and got one of the other fellows, the mining fellows,

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: and I told him about the property I had and everything and he says, I'll tell you, Mr. Sanders, he says, you take your crew and you cut all the trees and brush, he says, you don't have to pile it, but he says, just clear the right of way and he says, we'll come up and we'll put your road in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I says, what's it going to cost me?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He says, it won't cost you a cent. He says, you're the kind of men that we want to settle in the country here. And they come and put my road in, brought the dozers and the scrapers and things and put me a nice road, gravel road, all the way up. And I had a fine road right to it. And I had this property up on the mountain here, a copper property. It's a good property. And I went to the forestry and to the Milwaukee land and the state, it's a good property. And I said, I'll go and see them about putting in a road. And they give me the right of way, all right. But he says, to give you the right of way, he says, you've got to put your road 16 feet wide, the road bed, steel culverts in all the creeks and bridge the creeks and gravel it. Well, I says, man, if I had that kind of money, I says, I wouldn't want a road. Well, he says, we can't give you a right of way at all into the mine. He says, that's the specification that's got to be filled. And he says, you've got to keep track of the timber that you cut and you've got to salvage it and pay stumpage. Well, I says, Mr. Man, I says, that's just out of reason. I says, it'll cost $50 ,000 or more to put a road like that in there. Yes, he says, it will. Well, that's when I told him, I says, if I got this kind of money, I says, I wouldn't do it. And that was the difference between the two countries. Now, at that time, they'd sell, you could have bought timber up there for just a small round payment and they'd give you so much timber if you'd put in a sawmill and let them, they'd pay you $60 ,000 ,000 and they'd come and get the lumber at the mill and they'd give you all the timber that you wanted and they'd back you. You couldn't do that in this country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many people that you knew actually go up there and live and not come back?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yes, yes, there was lots of people went up there. There was lots of them that went up there, I forget their names now, they were strangers, but they'd left here and went up and several of them put in mills, little mills.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about yourself? What made you decide to stay here instead of try to make a go of it up there?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: When I lost this other property and I throwed up this other, I come back down here because

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I had put up a lot of money on this other property and I had to come back and get to work. I had a family come on home, so I never did go back.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: You know I wanted to ask you about the CCC's, that's something I hadn't asked you about yet and I know that you worked for the CCC camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did that come about that you started running the CCC's?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well that was in the Depression. You see the Depression and they didn't, when they shot those CCC's out here there wasn't men educated in the woods work, only from the men that they had right in the localities. And they'd give you an examination and they'd put you right into the forestry. Well that was pretty good money, that was $145 a month and bored. Where you wasn't getting, well they was getting $30 a month, these CCC's that we had in under us and bored.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And I worked every year until the Depression up until 1937. I run the IERA camps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The IERA, what's that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: That is a government camp. It was, they took on all the local men that were out of work and put them to work. Of course you,

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: there's a good deal like this that you can get a job with. There's road crews that they put on during the Depression too. You had to be off lenient with them and treat them with kid gloves because most of them, I had,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: what were they called?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He was the head lumber salesman for the Potlatch  when the Potlatch  shut down in 27 out at Spokane, office men. And I had him running, pulling the libiski . And bitter, talk about bitter. You never heard men say bitter as it was against the country. You see they lost their jobs and they come down there. I had him in as a crew leader. He got $45 a month and bored. I had captains fishing, captains, captains of the Navy, I had a crew captain from up Alaska there that was in there. And he said that's the best job he ever had in his life. And yet he was a captain on a, retired captain on a steam ship. He says I got my board here, I got a good bed. And he says I don't have to work hard and I can come home and read and he says my meals are all ready for me. And he says I've got nothing to worry about. He says it's the best job I ever had in my life. But he stayed till fishing opened up and then he had a fishing fleet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say pulling the libiski  s, what's that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: That's a wild gooseberry bush.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's what a libiski   is?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, a libiski  . A libiski  , oh. It's a gooseberry bush and a current bush. Ribes. Yeah, ribes. And the spore breed on those bushes and you pull them and pile them up and they die, you see. And it killed the spore. Well that is a bliss for us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These guys that were bitter against the country, they had been management themselves?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well they were. They was office men and never, they were, they were, they were, they were done a lick in their life. And they come out there and they thought they was badly abused.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But now this army captain, he said it was the best job that he ever had in his life and here they, one of the head lumbermen of the Potlatch  had just a small little bit of a salesman.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He was bitter against the way the country had gone. The way he was being treated. Yeah, the way he was being treated. He thought he was too good to be out doing common days work. Well, they.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your job as, were you running the camp actually?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your job to do? I mean how did you oversee these fellas?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, I'd take them out in different territories. I had two to three trucks, you see. You take about thirty in each truck or maybe more, take them out to the fields and they'd run strings. You had three workmen and then you had a crew leader and the crew leader would take this string ball, they had this twine in spools about that long and they'd run a line up twenty, thirty feet wide. Then the three workmen would, one would get in the middle and one here and the other here and they'd work out that line to the other end of it, you see. Maybe it'd be a half mile long. And your crew leader would come behind them and he'd seesaw back and forth and if they missed a libiski or gooseberry or anything why they'd pull them. Then the forestry had a checker and he'd come in after you got your territory worked out, he'd come in and he'd go through the same process through and if it had to pass by a certain percent, if it didn't by you had to work it again. Well that's what the CC's were doing, you see. Was cleaning these. Now up here at Collins we had a road cruise and they put roads up to Beulah's Butte and all through this country, good roads. And today you take the forestry and the fire patrol and the Potlatch  they've cut out. They don't maintain their roads anymore at all. Now you take even the Potlatch , all their side roads, they'll take a bulldozer and they'll bulldoze a ditch 8 -10 feet deep. Two or three of them across the road where they take off to keep you out of there. That's where the monopoly comes in on to the big concern. It won't be only just a few years till a man won't be allowed into the woods. They've put in there and got that now. The government put gates across different places here, locks on them and the Potlatch  started it but they made so much fun of them that old Richheimer opened them up. He was the superintendent here then. Well, the old iron gates you can go out on the East Fork and every side road has got big iron gates put up with railroad irons where they'd lock them to keep the people out of them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Interviewer - Bars, I wanted to ask you about the city kids that came out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Weren't there quite a few of them in the CCs that come from the East?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yes, I had letters from their folks and when they first came out the CCs didn't have any cooks. They just put green kids in there cooking.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: You ought to have seen some of the stuff that they'd take three of them at a stove and one would have bacon powder and they'd stir up flour and they'd make hotcakes. Well, the kids, there wasn't a decent hotcake. There was just, you might say, bacon powder and salt and water stirred up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Interviewer - Flour too? A little flour too?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh well, yes, flour. And I've seen as high as six of those fifty pound gallon garbage cans, you know, the big, I guess they're thirty gallons, garbage cans a day go out of those camps full of hotcakes. They wouldn't eat them. They would eat them. Well, the forestry wasn't supposed to say anything but we did. And we got cooks. A good cook from the Potlatch  that when they was shut down, they was cooks. We knew of them and in this territory we got the cooks in there and a little while they got them kids fed up and they had donuts and milk and cake along with their regular meals and they had meat two or three times a week. Well now, up on the Elks Butte in thirty -four, we had trouble with that camp there. They was going over the hill, that is going back home, beating their way back home. The brethren just stay and work at the camp. And George LaValley and myself, I was his assistant. We got with the captains and lieutenants of the army and told them that their grub wasn't sufficient for this kind of business out here, for climbing these hills. And they wouldn't believe it. Well, LaValley and I says, you come and go with us and spend just one day going up these mountains here, besides working as you go up the mountain, and see if you can get along. That won't bother us. They says in the morning we'll be right here and we expect you to go up with us.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And the next morning, here the captain and lieutenant and the mass sergeant was all right there. Well, when we started up the mountain, they walked a little ways and they stop and they puff. And George says, come on, he says, we've got to get up, get behind these kids. He says, they're up, already up there working. And here he says, you fellas are puffing and sweating. We pulled them about a quarter of a mile more and they was wiping the sweat off of their head. And he says, well, I don't believe we can do it. Climb this mountain. LaValley says just what I told you. Now he says, you expect these boys to get up here and work all day long and climb these mountains and they go up one string and down the other. And he says, on three sandwiches or two, he says, and they can't do it and they have to drink fine spring water and drink water. And he says, you fellas are sitting down there getting good pay and he says, these poor kids don't even have decent food. Well, they agreed right there. He says, we'll see if we can't do something about it. And they ordered milk in the little milk bottles and they went to baking donuts, making cake and they were all over the place. And they ate meat. Well, the first month they went way behind and they was going to stop it. LaValley says, just try it another month. And he says, see how you come out. Well, the kids got fed up, you see, and they commenced to eat less and eat less. And at last, while they could, on their allowance, they was allowed, I think, 57 cents a day apiece. And they come out fine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these kids ready to do that kind of work from the cities or were they not with them?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Of course, you couldn't,

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: it was all they wanted with those CCs is just keep them on the job. You couldn't get hard boiled with them or anything that way. You had to take them and treat them like a white person. I had a little boy named John, he had a little boy named John. I had safe breakers, what they called second story men, kids, kids out of the reform school, took out of the reform school, put them on probation, put them out here.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But you'd get them out onto the line and talk with them and go with them and pull the Levisky right along with them and treat them like a person. We didn't have any trouble with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they get along well among themselves or did they?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes, there was a lot of fighting. We had pugs, all kinds of pugs, kids with boxing gloves. We had, that is one of the main sports they'd put on every weekend. They'd have different camps fighting against one another. And some of them were pretty tough. Now, there was the first year there at Elk River, we had a bunch of Negroes. Well, they were tough. They'd take a ball bat or anything else and they'd wade right into the crowd and fight, knock them down and knock them unconscious. But we barred them. They had to bar them out, out of Elk River. Wouldn't let them come in. Well then they... They couldn't come into the crowd. On their days off because they was terrible. And they had camps over here on the other side of Santa and up from Clarkia that had the Negroes in. And boy, well I guess they'd come out of where they thought the whites was down on them, you see, because they was black. But they got out here and they had a different breed of whites. The white men never spleened against them at all. But back east they was hurt by the whites. They were murdered around in... It wasn't Hoboken, it was down in...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Out of the Negro settlement there in New York. Harlem. Harlem. They...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Harlem?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah. There was lots of them from Harlem and they were tough. But after they was out here a while, they'd come civilized. We called it civilized. But they were segregated out here. They were kept together. No. They weren't. They were mixed in the camps. Yeah. We had Negroes mixed all up. Some of them were fine lads you ever talked to.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: That's one thing the forestry wouldn't stand for is desegregating. You had to treat them all alike. But still if they couldn't come into town then they were... Well on the count of they caused much trouble. The towns spleened against them. Not only the blacks but the whites. The whites would get in it with the blacks. And they'd take the camps that they come from and they'd make them stay in camp. They'd compel them to stay in camp. That's the reason that so many of the kids jumped, went over what they called over the hill. They'd go back and the captains of the army went to firing them out, making them go over the hill. Firing them out of camp. Undesirables you see. And they'd... I turned loose up here at Squaw Meadows onto the army captain and I says, looky here mister. I says, you turn those fellas loose. No money. And make them bum their way back clear to New York.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I says, what are they going to do?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I says they're going to loot, rob or anything else to get money to get back home on. And I says the government says that you'll send them home. Undesirables you'll put them on the railroad and send them home. And I says that's what you're going to do. I says you're not going to take and send these kids over the hills. I says I've got a family, raising a family. And I says I'm not going to put up a guard at the house to guard the house. Now I says you fellas are going to be taking over the coals if you try to put them over the road or over the hill. Over the hill. That's what they call running them out of camp. They turned lots of them loose. And I had one fella on the elks butte and he said he come down, he liked me but he was an ornery kid. And he says Mr. Sanderson, he says I'm not going to work. He says I'm going over the hill tonight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well I says where do you live?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He says I live in Hoboken, New York. But he says getting here, that is before we got after them and made them feed the kids decent meals. Well I says I'll tell you what you do. I says just keep your mouth shut. But I says you come down when we come down at the truck tonight and tell me in front of all of these boys that you're not going to work anymore, you're going over the hill. And I refused to work. And I says I'll send you into camp. To the captain and he'll have to pay your fare. The government will have to pay your fare back home and you'll have three meals a day while you're going home. And he says all right. I sent a truck in with him before we got home. And I said I'll go out there. And the other three trucks is coming into camp. I hear the army captain come with him and they took him down to the railroad and put him on the four o 'clock train and sent him home. Give him a dishonorable discharge. But that didn't amount to nothing. But he got, now I had lots of them up on the east emerald. I had lots of them that I made them go into the captain or refused to work and go into the captain. They'd take them right down to St. Mary's and put them on the train and send them home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This boy in El Butte, was he black?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, no he wasn't black.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well this, why would they usually, why would a kid just say one at a time just decided they couldn't take it anymore? Was there usually a reason?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No there wasn't outside of the, well just think yourself how a young kid, they're always hungry. You take a young kid and they're always hungry. Well at that time they had these kids in there cooking and they couldn't cook. And I've, once a week they had meat in the camp. I've seen them just slice off a thin slice of raw beef and put it in one sandwich and gel on another and give it to them for the lunch. And I even had there at the camp, they pulled their lunch out and says looky here Mr. Sanderson, look at this meat here. Pulled it out, just raw beef steak, thin, in for lunch. Well I says let me see some of the other lunches and every one of them was that way. Well I stopped the truck right there, I says you're not going in the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the superintendent come around and he says what's the matter?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well I says Herb, I says look at these lunches.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I says would you eat it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he took them and looked at them and he made up a face, no he says I wouldn't eat them. He says take them back into the cook house and make them cook that meat. Well the kids in there, they didn't know any better. They'd never put up a lunch in their life before they come out here. I've seen those kids come out and they'd have shoes two times too big for them and the army give them pants that they take the upper part of the pants and pull it clear around over here and button and well you've seen these fellows on the stage where they had loose pants and clowns and stuff, well that's just what they looked like. And they didn't have any decent clothes until later after they got organized good. Then they commenced to get in clothes that didn't fit them. But they,

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: after we got them ironed out they was good kids. There was nothing wrong with them. They were just kids that, what they got to eat, the majority of them, what they got to eat they had to steal back in New York there, especially around Giddown. And that was why, that it was, well when the first load in they had, they had bread baked in long loaves about that long like this French bread. And they throwed it off of the coaches and the kids would catch them and they'd lay them right on the ground and pile them up just like cordwood. Then they were segregated out and taken out to each camp. And that's the way they had to feed them. This bread was hard -crushed you know, just like French bread. And I've seen stacks of bread twice three times as long as this room here piled up four or five feet high and five or six loaves wide. Whole coach of nothing but bread. Well that's what they made the sandwiches and stuff out of. It sounds like they were probably not eating too good when they came out of the hills. Well I had letters there that these kids that was going over the hills, they'd write home and tell them they was going to go over the hill and come home. You see they only got so much of their check and the other part the government gave to their family. And she says, one mother says, you think that you've got it hard out there you ought to be home here with us and there are fourteen, two families sleeping in a twelve by fourteen room. All we got is a curtain between us and we take turns cooking on the stove. And she says it's terrible. Well it was terrible. I had dozens of letters like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The kids would show you the letters?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes. Yes. Even the mothers would even write to the captain and the captain would bring the letters around and have us talk to the kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To try to get them to stay?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: To stay, yeah. Yes, I'll tell you. Them kids, I've had them tell me about, they'd steal, we'd steal anything he says that was loose. Go up to a fruit stand and one kid would go up and root the apples and things and they'd roll all over the street and they'd pick them up and away they'd go. That was the way they was getting something to eat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How were they, how did they get along in say like in Bowville or in Elk River in the towns? What would happen when the kids would come to town? What did the townspeople think of them?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well they was pretty skeptical to start with but afterwards they'd come in on a payday when they got their checks and they'd spend every nickel. Well it was no time at all until the town picked up and the kids picked up and just like putting the kids back to work. Well they was to work but it got the money circulating around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would they spend their money on in town?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They'd buy pop and sandwiches, places they bought cookies from, and they'd buy a lot of beer. They was lots of moonshine.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And the army captains and the army personnel, they was paid pretty good money. They was spending too. I've had kids who were in the town and come by here. The main road used to come right by the house here. And I had three, four milk cows and all we used was cream. And we kept some milk to drink. But I've had kids come by out here and on a Sunday I always made two or three five gallon freezers of ice cream. I always had a big bunch. There's nothing to have twenty five thirty here at the house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Of the CC kids?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, no of the town kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Town kids?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: It's hard times and the CC kids would come by here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hey mister, can we turn that freezer for you?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: If you'll give us some ice cream. I says you're just a huckleberry as I'm looking for. Get over here. And they'd come over and sometimes I'd make two ice creams freezers full, five gallons. Freezer, one of them big freezers you know. Boy they'd turn till the sweat would just be rolling off of them. Hot. Some of them would eat two, three dishes of ice cream and I'd fill them up.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: You know at that same time the kids town, they were starting to death before that CC started here. Lots of families. The family lived right up on the hill here from me. All they had was flour and salt when I found it out. And I was a cattleman. And we had our own cattle and we'd butcher and we'd give lots of them meat. And I was cutting wood and Damon was hauling when the Potlatch  had four or five hundred horses here and he'd bring in a load of oats for the Potlatch . And we'd bring in a load of to feed the horses and he'd take a load of wood. He had a low boy and he'd take eight cord of wood. Well I got two and a quarter a cord for sixteen inch wood out on the road and he'd load it up. He'd take it down to Genesee  and around in there. He lived down there. Well old Bill Damon, I guess you knew him. And he'd trade it to the farmers and get coffee and sugar and lard and coffee. I've had coffee come up twenty pounds at a time. Sugar two, three hundred pounds at a time. And that's why I couldn't get no cash. I got just enough to keep my wood saw running. Damon gave me that much. But Damon never got money out of it. He'd take trade. Once in a while he'd get money. So you'd get some of that trade too. Well yes. He'd take and buy my wood. Of course he probably got six, seven dollars a cord for the wood. But you couldn't sell wood here that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he was giving you two fifty and two and a quarter in cash and then besides that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, no cash.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just in trade?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Just in trade. I had a root house out here. I had twenty sacks of apples at a time.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Twenty and you could buy a sack of spuds if you had the money for thirty five cents. But you didn't have the money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How hard was it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: You had told me before about some of the other families but I was thinking about how hard it was for your family in the twenties and then in the depression.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it get a lot harder for your family to get by?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well yes. It made it hard because you didn't have any cash. Now your light bills. You see your light bills was almost the same as then as they are today.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Just a few things like that is what you had to have cash for. Well it was hard to have to raise any cash and other families didn't have. The Washington Water Power cut off family after family in here because they didn't have the money to pay for their lights.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well once the CCs came in did that make a big difference to the whole area?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: It made a wonderful difference. That's what turned it around. That's what turned it. Right from the time them CCs come in prosperity started to pick up. It wasn't no time at all after the CCs got here that people commenced to get a little money and they commenced to flourish.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They've got the same thing right today. If the unemployment bureau would stop and the food stamps would stop we'd go within two weeks time we'd be right into a civil war. There no question to it because they won't take it like they did during the other depression.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come you think they won't take it anymore?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Because these young kids are you can see what they do. You call them hippies and all of that but if they didn't get their food stamps they're not going to starve to death that's a dead mortal sink and they're going to get it the best way they can. You take the monopoly of these big companies you take Safeway Store and Rosauers  and you they've drove all of the little stores around in the country they've drove them out. And you take the Potlatch  and these logging outfits they've bought 90 percent of all the timber in here. Well they've got timber and cedar and stuff that they won't use for a hundred years it stood there for a hundred years. And you go through and they've taken the cream off of the country but this other stuff is standing there and they'll never go back and get it in the world. They're even today they're taking young stuff, steady day matured timber and this other stuff is standing there. Well the little cedar fella he made a going out and getting cedar he made a sale for it. Now the Potlatch  is buying all of these little shingle mills and things out that'll sell to them and they're duplicating what they've made and now they call them cedar thieves because they're trying to make a living. They haven't got work. Now they used to be the Potlatch  had two three thousand men right here at Bovill  working in the woods. And today they've got forty fifty men that are here. Here's kids downtown well all over the country and they're trying to get permits by the cedar to make cedar and they won't sell the government won't sell them any cedar unless they put down a thousand fifteen hundred dollars. If they had a thousand fifteen hundred dollars they wouldn't want that permit. But to make cedar they're trying to get permits get work and then they wonder what their generation's going to come to. The Potlatch  won't sell them any timber or they won't buy any cedar. So you figure that the hard times now has got a lot to do with the monopoly that's going on. Well here's the Potlatch  they've got hundreds of acres back east there that they're clear cutting and they can raise soybeans and stuff onto that ground and make more and they make on their timber and that's what they're doing. And they're working right against the farmer. Well that's making hard times. And you take right here. Yesterday I was out I run over a hundred miles out through the woods and the roads are terrible. Worse than they used to be. Yeah oh they don't maintain them at all anymore. I went into ditches that I just barely crawl down in and go up and just a few roads that you can get through on. And you take I went through places that they're cedar that they've cut the timber and they're just cedars all over. Well they never go back in the world and get that cedar. And yet you take right here in town this town that flourished and go ahead just leaps and bounds if they turn these companies and put it sell it to them on small bases and make them clean it up and let them market their their timber. Now here's fellas here is right down here that got the long butts off of the cedar poles of the cedar company here. And they make a few of them are making a hundred dollars a day.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Here they've got iron gates the forestry's got iron gates on the roads and the Potlatch  had iron gates on all their roads. And they they they they they they they they they they when they get through going in like a going into a certain part of the road outside of the main road that goes in there like in the on flood wood all the side roads that go up the canyons and things they take a dozer and they dig a ditch eight and ten feet deep two or three of them across the road so you can't go in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that doing?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Just exactly that they this woods stood here millions of years thousands of years before the Potlatch  or them ever got into it and it never burned up at that. That's one of the hooks that they these companies use to keep the people out of the woods. There was thousands of people go up on the Dubuque and pick huckleberries the roads is washed out and they can't get up there today. Well there were lots of people all over the country liked pick huckleberries same way with the the garnet digging. The garnet is a mineral I can take the mineralogy and it's a form of beryllium and they locate emerald mines and things all over the world gold mines and yet the forestry our government closed it up and yet they take it and they release you a little piece of ground and let you dig and you only can take a certain amount of garnets out but they want five dollars a day and then you've got to fill the hole back up and somebody else will dig. Well that's not right it's a public domain to start with.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what I'm wondering about is do you think that the common man is in a worse position now than he was forty fifty years ago when the depression hit?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yes there are no comparison at all. You take forty fifty years ago and you could come out and take a homestead and you could you could prove up on it and sell it for a certain amount or you could make a living on it. These fellas had put in little mills right here Sam Frye had a hundred and sixty acres up here he put in a little sawmill and he he had a little didn't get much for his lumber but he he made raised a nice big family and he always had had acreage enough that he could run a cattle and the cattle my folks sold nice big three year old steers twenty five dollars apiece. Well today you're that same steers bring four hundred anyhow and you take all of these little farmers and things that was through the country here. Derry was Troy all of those towns fifty sixty years ago were made but today if you strike a good mine up here on the mountain I had a copper mine up here if you would make a million dollars the government would take half of it and then to run the mine and get the other the state and them all paid off and everything you wouldn't have any money but Spokane when the Coeur d 'Alene's discovered if a man made a million dollars he come in to Spokane and put up the old Paulson building and he put up a lot of big buildings there he had the money to put it in but now the government will take it away from you. Do you really think that you know I think about that they talk about rags to riches in the old days they used to think you know there was that rags to riches was a real thing that could happen but I kind of wonder because maybe a few people made it big but it seems to me that most people always had to fight to make a good living. Well they did they didn't really fight in this country to make a living they was well you take in the old in the early day they depended on thrashing a lot well your grocery bill most of the grocery stores now like in Pluess old Frederick and Fuzzy and all of them old time merchants would carry the common family for five hundred dollars through the year well during thrashing season and other work they'd pay up their grocery bill that's the way they lived that is the common people but the rancher he'd haul his wheat in and the warehouses and handle the wheat for them and they always come out all right. You could go out to those ranches and they all lived good and the average person lived good because you could buy a sack of flour for thirty five forty cents.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about hobos? Weren't there quite a few guys around come through the country that were?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Not at that time there wasn't they was the hobo was more into the time of the depression time the railroads got into this country and they commenced to be what they called the hobo.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were those guys like?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Just like these young hippies going from town to town. Some of the best workers I've ever seen were hobos. Some of the best floaters they called them floaters a little later on they was floaters titty hobos. Some of the down here in the twenties for the Potlatch  they'd come in the shop and show some of their welding with settling and once in a while you'd strike one with an art welder but they were good working. Well they'd work on this job and on that job and they had the experience but the hobos we had a jungle down here and they would be forty fifty men in the jungles. Well they'd all chip in together one would buy bread the other would buy something else in the summer time like when the corn was on the market and stuff like that. They'd take an old five gallon oil can cut the top out of it and fill it up with water and take the corn and fill it up. Well the fellow that had ten cents would buy a loaf of bread and they'd all go together and they'd have a good meal. I've seen them lots of times and they didn't have anything on the old tin cans to keep the rain and shelter in off of them and sleep and maybe spend a week there if they had a bunch that had a little money and earned a little bit they'd all want to buy one thing and the other thing and they'd go together. Well that's what they called the lumberjacks. There wasn't so many lumberjacks. They'd get broke and they couldn't get work and they'd go up to the hobo camp and with a few nickels they had they could buy a loaf of bread or something and go in and they'd eat. And they had their at that time they packed their own beds with them and they'd lay down they'd take the boughs off of the trees down in here and they'd fix them a bed on top of the boughs. And they'd take their own boughs. But they they wasn't they might steal a chicken now and then if they saw a chicken out but they I've seen them walk along the little wheat in their pocket and throw it out and the chicken would come up and pretty soon they'd reach down and they'd have the chicken. Well a man gets hungry he's gonna get it one way or another. Right today I was thinking when I was going through the woods if the Potlatch  and the diamond and the Milwaukee would turn loose of their cedar little little jobs so these little fellas could handle it they'd be making cedar all over and these other outfits handle their their post and stuff they sell it out and the town would flourish not only here but all over wherever they you get the circulation of money that's what trouble of our country right today they're no jobs here for these young fellas. And if them big fellas in Washington DC would go through now the the Potlatch  hollers are they spoiling the timber and making it impassable when they had the donkeys in there I've seen them knock trees down and ditches where they'd pull the main lines in 15 -20 feet deep and you go through there today and you can't find a ditch it's all filled right back up and well that's in within a 50 year time limit and what little that they would do if they'd put their government inspectors and have them the other inspectors come through that sells them the timbers and see the fire association see that they clean up their their ground and pile it and clean it up before they give them another strip it had put the men to work and they'd take them off of the streets. Do you remember, I'm sure you do, I was going to ask you a little what Bovill  was like when it was a flourishing town back in the teens in the early 20s. I've seen five or six hundred people in that part down there and there was lots of money there was more money more money at that time of floating when the wages was two and a half three dollars and four dollars top. Well there was more money in the town then than there have ever been since. Now we had two thousand men working in the woods and some of them would stay there a month or two months and then they'd come into town in a couple of weeks they were broke and they'd go back and go to work.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But now to show you the town down here we had you see we had three hotels and they was always full and we had Grant and Giles and we had E .K. Parker and we had the Grobens Brothers and we had two butcher shops A .A. Anderson had a barber shop with three chairs in it and you could never get out of there before twelve one o 'clock at night. All was full. The barber shop. The barber shop and we had a dentist and we had a drug store. Mrs. Crawford had one of the finest drug stores there was in this country and we had a bank. Well there was at one time there was three butcher shops here and we had three pool rooms and they was all making money and the town had a band. They give the town twelve hundred dollars the businessmen did among their souls to start this band. I played in the band myself and they wanted to give me a little money to get out of here and they'd buy suits for the ball players. They'd buy the club all suits and if they wanted to raise any money to help out any other way we had excursions from St. Mary's and we had Moscow. Our band played against contest against Moscow and Potlatch. All the towns had a little band and there was always something doing.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: We had the governor several times down here in the park and down to make speeches. They had a good deal like Troy putting on their Troy day. The old timers.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But today all you got is saloons. You haven't even got a grocery store. You have to go to Moscow to get groceries. Yeah, they're at a grocery store. Yeah, in the back end of the saloon there and you go in and they got a half a dozen different articles that's all. You can't buy anything that you want.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Bread and sugar and butter but you can't get nice fresh vegetables or anything that way.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But you let them stop given the unemployment and the food stamps and we're going to have it. They're going to have hard times. That's the only thing that's keeping the country up right now. It's the food stamps and the unemployment. You see we didn't have that in the other depression. We didn't have the unemployment. You had to get out and make it. If you didn't you starved. I seen the Potlatch  have down here have a whole warehouse full of when they shut down of gallon cans of fruit and flour and sugar and coffee with the tons of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think they'd put it out to the people of the town?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No they wouldn't. An old noggin he sold it to other fellas that had the money for $25. Three big truckloads for $25 put it out because he said it was going to spoil. Instead of giving it out to the people that needed it and letting them have a chance at it. Hubert Hall is the one that bought the most of it and he turned around and made five times out of it selling it to other people.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He wouldn't sell to anybody that didn't have the money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think most people held on here during the depression? Did most people stay rather than leave?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No they stayed. You couldn't leave. They didn't have no transportation. They didn't have cars like they got now. They couldn't buy gas before they did have. I think your sister told me of a story that you told about one of these army men in the CC camps that didn't have thought that a fire was a sunset.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that story?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well that was up on Elks Butte where I was the assistant to George Lavallee in the forestry.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: We got that camp set up. We had a nice camp and these fellas were from the east. Well they'd never practically seen mountains until they came out here through the west.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: All the CCs and their personnel came running up to the forestry shacks or tents and hollered and fire, fire, fire. Well of course that is one thing we was on the alert for in the hot weather.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: We bounded out of our tents in the evening and we asked them where it was. They said down around the bend. Well we went down there and the sunset on the side of the mountain was just all red. It was just like you had big flashlights throwing in there. Beautiful. One of the most beautiful sights you ever looked at.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: George, I was with George, he says where's the fire? Why he says can't you see it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He says the whole country's a fire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: George looked at him and he says man, don't you know what that is?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yes, I know what that is. That's a forest fire. Well he says that's the sunset on the side of the mountain. He says that's not unusual here. He says we have that in the mountains all the time in the fall. And his countenance dropped and he looked. Well, he says I've never seen anything like that. He says you just have to forgive me for saying that as a fire. He says I've never seen anything like that. Well, born with a gold spoon in their mouth and never seen anything like that. Well, there was 250 CCs all lined up there. We was getting ready to fight fire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did these boys stay out for very long, the CCs?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes, they stayed till the snow all practically. The last camps went out just before September.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then they went back home?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yes, they took them back home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would the same kids come out the next year or so?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes, lots of them did. Yeah, lots of them stayed out in this country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh really?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: The results of the CCs. My daughter married one.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But I've seen them poor CC kids when they come out here. They had shoes that the Army would give them. Some of them were too small for them. They'd take a jackknife and cut out parts of it around to loosen up the shoes so it wouldn't hurt their feet. And after they was out here for a month, why, they commenced to get shipping clothes in that would fit them. But that's what Roosevelt done is to get them off of the streets and around these cities. That's the reason that the majority of them were kids out of the cities, is to get them off of the street.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were these kids when they first got here?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I had kids there 14 years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That young?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah. I had lots of kids. I was only 17.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Steel?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: When they first come out here, the cookhouse, they'd unload, been unloading a truck with grub. And the kids that was on detail, they'd throw grub off of there and they'd grab it and take it out in the woods and they'd hide it and then they'd go up and eat. Well, they were starved to death. But after they was out here a month, six weeks, why, they didn't do that at all. They commenced to spruce up and kids got fat on the job.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But I'll tell you, it was a pretty hard thing. There was a little town up here, Slabtown. There were families up there that all they had was a sack of beans that some farmer had given. I went up there and inquired and found out about different families.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But I'll tell you, the Herman boys, when I found them, all they had was just flour and salt and water to mix up and make gravy out of that too. To eat on their hotcakes and three times a day.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Then I took them and get them to help me with wood. Couldn't get no money out of the wood but we could trade it in. Where I traded it in that way, why, they commenced to get stuff to eat.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Of course, we had the place here and we had milk cows and we had beef. You didn't need to shut your cattle up at all. We had the whole range. But now the cattlemen have got in and they've got all the range and your cattle you've got to keep them closed in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to ask you, was your wife a local person?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, she was a local woman. She was from Rockford up here by Coeur d 'Alene. And then her folks moved down here. But that was in the late 20s. That is just before the Depression really started out in this country. The Depression started at that time back in the East. It was back East there four or five years before it ever hit the West. But it gradually hit us. The Potlatch  in 30... I think it was 30...33, shut down completely, broke. Then after they started up, to start up they had to borrow a million dollars to get started.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But after the CCs came in, now the businessmen from the CCs left. They'd go and buy these silver dollars at the bank and they'd get a thousand or two thousand dollars worth of them. Billy Watts and them. And they was broke before the CCs come, but after the CCs this year.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They got to going.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they made lots of their money that way. Now the CCs had never seen a silver dollar.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they'd pay a dollar and a half for a silver dollar. Well they'd go to the bank and get two thousand of them and in four or five days the two thousand of them was gone. They was buying them souvenirs. They'd pay a dollar and a half for them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I've seen Billy Watts come up with sacks. A canvas sack. Five hundred dollars in a sack. And they'd sell them out before night. Or if you'd get a little garnet. These kids, that's what they'd spend their money on for souvenirs. And they'd buy it. Lots of them went to prospecting. And they'd get a little chunk of gold and then they'd sell it to the other kid.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: That's just what they need right today is something to put these kids to work.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I get the Potlatch paper every week... They put out all their loading rigs and chaise. Marions.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I didn't want the master mechanic. I was offered the master mechanic. But there was too much grief. At that time you had to do the... Look after all of these machines. My father was, I guess, the last real master mechanic that they ever had. But these other fellows that was put in as a master mechanic, they wasn't a master mechanic. They was just an office man, that was all. To keep the books. And each department had their... Now the motor department had their head mechanic. And in my department I had four chaise, two rod engines, five Marions, and one McGifford. Well, any time they'd break down they'd just phone in and I'd have to go out and locate the trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your department called?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, I was boiler maker and Ernie Smith was over his shop, or over the lays and stuff that way. And the motor crew was over their end of it. Then they had the electrician shop and the blacksmith shop, welding shop.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And if there was a welding job or anything, the office crew would come in and tell the department such and such a job. You had to go out onto the job and fix it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who did you take your orders from? Who was your boss?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: The superintendent.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They did have... Freel was the best old mechanic that the Potlatch  had for the logging end of it. Then they put Johnny Grow in, but he was no mechanic. He was just an office fellow. And something would break down where he'd come and tell you so and so needs help and you'd have to go out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you join a union when they got one?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember when they finally did get a union?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I think that was in... ...44, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that CIO?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: AFL.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it AFL?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah. And later it was turned into the CIO.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much opposition from the company at that time to getting the union here?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: There was for quite a while. It took quite a lot of these labor union leaders to break Richheimer in. They'd... Now, Curly Wonderlicke. Richheimer fired him right off the job because his brakes wasn't up on his truck. And he was... had a halfway or runaway. Well, the labor union, he went to the labor union over it. The labor union went over and checked the truck that next night. And the brakes shouldn't have been... the truck shouldn't have been allowed out onto the highway at all. Well, it wasn't the driver's fault that the brakes was that way because he wasn't a mechanic on the... And that's... Richheimer fired him because he had that little runaway.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Old Swarchman, he says, How many trucks you got are roadworthy?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He says they're all roadworthy. He says, You haven't got a truck. If the law had shut down on you, it would go out onto the road. And he took them around and showed them. And old Richheimer pulled in his horns and he hired... put Curly back on and paid him for the time he was off. And that's the way the Potlatch  got into the union. It was through forcing of the union labor. They had a strike down here at the shop.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And... They had the shop practically shut up. Well, the Potlatch  stayed with the men. And any man had been with them for five or ten years steady.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Why, they made the union... the union couldn't claim them. Well, there was a bunch of them on the start that... Until we got to see what the union was. And... They didn't go in. But after they found out what the union was, why, they all went in. And then the Potlatch , they... They got this... Insurance started.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And... The Potlatch  got the insurance on their men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are you doing?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the four L's at the end of the First World War?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I heard that was just a company union. It was more or less a company union.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But... That's the reason the four L's quit is because they made a... The Potlatch  made a regular company union out of it. The Potlatch  was always square with their labor. But they didn't want the union. But as far as the Potlatch ...

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Why... If you worked for the Potlatch , they created you a fine. That's the reason it was so hard for the union to get started. And because the Potlatch  always went along with other countries. Around, if the wages had raised in the other country, the Potlatch  had raised. That's one thing you can say for the Potlatch , that they was... They was awfully good with their men. I did hear that when they shut down at Bovill  here, that there's quite a few guys that were too short of retirement that got canned. Yeah. Yeah. That didn't sound... No, that wasn't good either. No, and they cut them out. They cut them out when they shut down. I, for myself, was... I worked 52 years. But during the Depression, there was a stretch in there that we had to work for ourselves, all the men. Because the Potlatch  didn't have work. When they started up, they didn't have work to put all their full crews back.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And... They wouldn't count that along in onto your... Of course, that was... It wasn't the Potlatch , I'll tell you. It was their men that thought they was running the Potlatch .

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He wouldn't give a local boy a chance to go to work, but yet let a stranger come in and ask him for it, and he'd put him right out on the job.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Now, I went to work for the seedy yard. Well, the seedy yard was the Potlatch  stone seedy yard. I worked there for three, four years.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And yet... He says that don't count. He says that he wasn't working for the Potlatch . Well, I says it was the Potlatch . Well, he says they don't figure it that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This is towards a pension?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah. Consequently, I got beat out of five years on... After I was 65, and I got beat out about five years before. That's the reason I... From the Potlatch  pension, I get $39 a month. I was too old, you see, when it started, to get a good pension out of it. Ernie Smith was too old. He got $33, I think. He worked 52 years for them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Now, I was going to ask you about Clarkia a little bit. Because I had heard that Clarkia was really a wide open town in the early years. That was. That there was more prostitution and bootleg there than probably any place around the country. Well, there wasn't any more than there was here at Bovill.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Nobody's here. They had two big sporting houses here. And then they had the several women in the hotels, too.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But... They was all, all of these towns. The St. Mary's. And then there was another little town this side of St. Mary's. And then there was Santa. Fernwood. Clarkia. And Elk River. They all had... their houses and wide open. You could buy any liquor or anything else that you wanted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the women would stay in one house for a long time?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They'd move around from one town to another. No, they stayed in one house. They was just like a family. You take up at Elk River and them girls is there five, six years till they got them out. Same way down here. They was... They'd stay right there. And they... They had a doctor for the health. Right there, pretty regular.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Then they put them out and they went to the hotels. And went to Spokane. That's the reason the lumberjacks always went to Spokane. So they'd been here in the earlier years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened? Did they finally get kicked out by the town?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yeah, the town kicked them out. Got rid of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that because of the churches probably?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, I think it was. It cleaned the town up.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Remember prohibition was on too in that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they kind of go together?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To move them?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah. Yeah, they'd all go out together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Byers, did you think yourself when you got married and started a family here, did you figure out that you just were going to stay and raise them right here? Or did you think much about going elsewhere where they might be going?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I figured on staying here. You see, I started in 1912 working for the Potlatch . And I always had steady work.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I did one time in 1914. World War I. I put my application in at Bremerton. And I wasn't called until 1916. And I went over and I worked a while at Bremerton. And then I went into the Navy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you break any glass off last night?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I went into the Navy. I was working in the Navy. I got an awful balling out for going into the Navy in Spokane. I come home on a vacation. I went into the Navy up here. I'm home to them there. And the old adjutant, he says, I'll tell you, Byers, he says, we need men here twice as bad as we need them on the front or out on the boats. He says, for order the Navy to carry on, we've got to have the mechanics. You see, I was experienced into the boiler work. And that's one of the first jobs I took up was boiler work for the Potlatch . I worked with a boiler maker for years to start with that and blacksmith work. And, of course, when I went there at Bremerton and put in as boiler maker, as soon as the opening come, they sent right for me. And he says, we need the mechanics here twice as bad as they need them on the boats or on the front. He says, we can get all the men we want that way. But he says, the men with the experience, we haven't got.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I made an awful mistake by not joining.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, if you wanted to join the Navy, why didn't you join here?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But I never got out of the Navy Yard if I had it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long were you in then? When did you? Until for four years?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, yeah, I was in for seven years.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Seven years, I was joined up for four years. And then they put us in reserve for three years or four years. I forget which it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see any action in the first war?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, I didn't get to go over that. Just before the armatures were signed, our company was due to ship out to France. But the armatures cut it out. And then 30 days after the armatures were signed, we was all home. They sent us all home. That's one thing they done right off the relay. Turned 1 ,000, there's 25 ,000 in our camp. They're on the Gulf of Mexico. They sent us all home.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: That cut down the big expense on the government, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are you doing? What are you doing?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But if they don't do something with these large companies getting monopoly on things, we're going to see some hard times. And some of the big fellows are commencing to notice that now.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Now, not only the Potlatch , but these other concerns.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, like your computers. Look at the office people. That put out of work. One building alone there in New York, they put over 5 ,000 technographers off of the job. Just out of one building. One of those big office buildings. Well, you take all over the United States, look at the men, that people that put off work, they had to find other jobs. Now they haven't got the jobs. Automation has taken hold of, like the Potlatch  here. Now they've got a loader, these, I call them crawdad machines. They'll, what the full crew and the train crew and handling the logs and everything, one man, on that loader will do the work of 10, 15 men. And do away with two or three shaves. Now one man, two men down here, take those trucks that come in, and it only takes four or five minutes to unload a whole truckload of logs. Where it used to take all kind of time before. You know, one thing that strikes me, and you talk about what some of the bosses were like, it seems to me that there were a lot of two -bit bosses. Yeah, there was. There was a lot of two -bit bosses, and they are yet today.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think make those guys be like that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Goes to their head, they get to think they're a boss, goes to their head. Now, Wallace Bovill, he quit here, he was a log loader on the Marion. I fixed his Marion year after year for him. And he went over on the other side, and he was a nice fella. And they worked him in as a boss, and then he got to be the superintendent. Well, or assistant superintendent.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And his train boss first, he got to be a train boss.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And lining the train crews out, three, four engines, you know, and they'd line them out to different camps where they had to go out and switch them out. And he went hog wild. He commenced calling the men sons of bitches and everything else. And the superintendent raked him over the coals, told him if you carry on like that, he says, we'll have to get rid of you. He says, this job isn't a king's job at all. He says, you've got, those men's got feelings just as well as anybody else. And he says, you've got to treat them as such. Well, that was Blackberry that bawled him out.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they made a fine man out of him. But he's retired now. But it went to his head.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah, Walt Hornby was another one of them. I knew most all of those old fellows. Lots of them were there. Now Axel Anderson, it was altogether different. And he, he'd, he was like the old time bosses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what are you, what are you doing?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I see you're not doing anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you, what do you figure on doing?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, I'm looking for work. Well, go up to Camp Sojetsa. Tell them I sent you up there. They'll find something for you. The way they'd go, they'd go to work. But these other fellows, unless you got down and, and begged them for a job like Chet Angle down here. He thought he was part of the company. But when he, after he, they took this office away from him downtown and put him up here as his common clerk into the main shop. He called them chicken shit. He says, the way the company's getting now, he says, I don't even want to work for them. Well, he got his shingle cut from where he thought he was something, you know, and, and he found out he wasn't anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you like a cup of coffee?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah. Sure. I'll be going pretty quick anyway. I've been here quite a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you use cream and sugar?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, just black. So, yeah, it's a funny thing because you would think that these, that guys would want to help the town or help the people that they knew.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, my brother, son -in -law, he went in and he says, is there anything open, Chet?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, he says there's no job open. He says, they're not putting any men on. Well, Richheimer was in the, in the office and he, he looked out and he seen Euler. He says, Euler, he says, I've got a job for you. Put him on a job right up here where they needed a top loader. One of the best jobs around. Made Chet madder than a header.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And my boy went in time after time and, no, nothing showed up. Well, his kids come from the university that didn't know sick 'em and Chet put them right on. So, Max, he went to, to Richheimer. He says, Richheimer, he says, I've asked Chet for over three months for a job. And he says, he puts on these college kids and won't give a town kid a job. Well, Richheimer says, you go up to the shop and you go into the welding shop. Max was a welder. And he went in there and he worked for two, three years steady at welding.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Chet, he says, why didn't you come to me?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Says, going over to, to Richheimer. He says, I did come to you for over three months and he says, you, every time I come in there, there wasn't no job, wasn't no job. He says, right after he told me there was no job, I went to Richheimer and he put me right up there in the shop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember Melker Anderson, Axel's brother?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I heard he was a good foreman. He was a fine foreman, one of the finest men they had. Axel was a fine foreman. Billy Watts was a fine foreman. And Larry Murphy was a fine foreman. All them old foremans, they were working men themselves and they knew what it, what it was to be a foreman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the difference?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that's the difference?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I think it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The good foreman didn't forget what it was to be a working man?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah. Now old Hector McFarland, he was a, he was a railroad man, steel gang, before he ever come out to this country. And he was used to handling foreign Greeks and Serbians and Bulgarians and a white man wouldn't work for him when he come out. Well, he, he'd cuss the men out and swear at them, call them all the foul names that they could think of. And they'd just mourn and dance around to his tune.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And this Dick Kamer's brother, er, wife, his wife's brother went to work for him. He was a white man. And the first time old Kamer and McFarland called him a son of a bitch, they had a fight on their hands. He went and he just knocked the supreme socks right off him. He says, you fire me over this job, off of this job. And he says, I'll give you another lickin'. And they kept him too. But he, he done a lot of good. They cut out that rough talk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, why is it that you didn't want to be a big shot yourself?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Why didn't you want to be a, you say you didn't like the headache. No, because there was too much, too much er, grief to it.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: You, you, now, you see you had the warehouse and you had the ordering. And er, you had the book work. All of those things one man had to do. And if you was the master mechanic, that's what you done. You took our, ordering all the material. And you took care of the book work. And you took care of your crews, er, a workin'. In the machine breakdown you had to know what to tell them to do. And it was just, my father used to get up at four o 'clock in the morning and get up at five o 'clock, on to the, the book work at five o 'clock. And by seven o 'clock he'd have his book work done and take over and line the crews out. And then he'd have to go into the warehouse and figure out what they had to, and I used to tell him. I says, you've got men on the job, they're drawing just as much money as you're getting. And they don't have to get up that time in the morning and get on to the job or they don't have the grief that you've got. And I says, I wouldn't have a, a job like that for no money. I wouldn't.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: They kept their best cat man there was in this country. And he knew just exactly when a machine break down what to do and everything else. And he had the whole thing in under him. But yet when they changed these foremen, superintendents around, and they put Joe Parker in down here as a superintendent, that is because Parker had, was a, a moneyed man. And he wasn't a mechanic at all. But he took, because he was over the mechanic, and they got rid of Freel because a new boss wanted new bosses. Well, every man on the job was sore over the job, over Freel. And they, when they got rid of Freel, they give him a pick up and told him to go out and look around and see if he could find a job at some of these other companies. After he'd been here 20, 25 years, taking care of their cats and keeping them on the job, and they had 50 some cats at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he was good, right?
