speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He knew his business. He knew more in a minute than any man they've ever had since. And they put Johnny Zaglow in there that, he wasn't a mechanic at all. But he understood he'd read anything and remembered. And these big shots from Lewiston come up and they'd hear Johnny talk, and they thought he knew what he was doing. But, he got, at the last, they changed this fellow from Spokane, Deer Park, come down, took over. And, uh, the other, De Ward was one of them. And they bought Johnny, after Johnny had been 5 or 10 years a master mechanic under him. They bought him a set of tools and told him to go out in the woods when a machine break down, you go out there and repair it. Well Johnny come to me and he says, you know, Byers, he says, they know that I can't go out there and repair a machine out in the woods. Well I says, no. I says, no other mechanic is going to go out there and repair them. I says, the shop is what they built here to repair their machine. Now they want you to go out there and repair a machine that is broke down and you've got to have cranes and everything else to lift them and get it around. And I says, it's just that they want to get rid of you. Sure enough, I, they, they bought another man and put him in there to oversee the shop while Johnny would go out. He went out once or twice and then he says, I can't take it. And he felt bad and they took him to the hospital but his heart exploded before he got to the hospital and killed him. He was sick and they knew he'd been doctoring for several years.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I didn't like that at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to Freel? Did he find another job?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh he went down to Lewiston and Lewiston put him on, not the Potlatch , but those welding shops. He was a welder. I learned welding in under him and the government man years ago. Seems like you take people that have been in, say like people living here for years, and then they got no choice. If the company should fire them, then they got to go. Well, the shop, the men was, they called it a strike but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Over Freel?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Over Freel and we all talked him out of pulling the strike. It was no good because Freel said he wouldn't come back anyhow. But there was five or six that had mechanics quit over that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Quit? They actually quit and didn't come back?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, George Bell and several of them quit, wouldn't come back at all.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Went over and made a shop there in Fernwood, put up a machine shop and these gyppo dealers all over the country. He made more money than he ever thought of making here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these guys that worked under Freel or guys that worked with him?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, they worked under Freel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were his mechanics?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah, they were good mechanics.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And then when they brought this fellow from De Ward here, why, they're good mechanics, walked away from them because they changed the men around. They took De Ward, put in Rosie a day over the shop after Johnny... Johnny... Rosie never was a mechanic.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He had the gift of gab and that's what De Ward liked. He had the gab and...

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He... But Harry Glacier, Bernhoudt and all the electrical engineer and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, what was that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Head man in the motored division. They all quit and left. They said they wouldn't work on the room at all.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: When you... I want to ask you just a little bit about your family, you know, when you were raising your kids. Because it's something that we're, you know, I'm real curious about is how these things have changed, if they've changed through time, you know, the way they are now and the way it used to be. Because kids seem kind of different now than they were 50 years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you spend a lot of time with the family?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Did you have... Oh yes, I had all the time with my family right here, you see. I worked... That is one thing with my job, I was always right at home every night.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: That's one reason that you take all the little kids in town. Well, during the depression there, there was one fourth of July that they never had a dime. And the people never had a nickel to put out for tall. Well, I had an old truck. I took two tubs, three tubs I guess. Went up to foot into the heavy brush up by Beulah's Butte and got three tubs of ice on the fourth of July. That was snow and ice. I brought it down and I could buy this pop that we're buying today. I could buy it five cents a bottle. Well, I bought a couple hundred bottles of pop. And I bought four or five boxes of weenies in a box about that long, about that wide.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And buns. And I had a grill out here.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I got the grill going and I put water in these tubs and put the pop in there. And I got every little kid in town.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And I, the wife and two or three of the women in town, their mothers, helped the grill run the grill, you know. And roast the weenies and put them in buns and they'd have pop and ice cream. Made several big five gallon cans of ice cream because I had the cows.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you know those kids?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: There are several of them live in town here yet and they've got kids. Some of them going to college today, they remember that. They tell it very often.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just to have the fourth of July with pop?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, pop and hot dogs and ice cream. They played games. They'd take a stick and hold it over the grill and fix their own sandwiches. But I had any kind of pop they wanted, you know. Ice cold. Well, they didn't have a thing in town. Nobody had any money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel you were strict with your kids or lenient when you raised them?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, I was probably pretty strict with them. Oh, I got a crank. No, I was pretty strict with them. As far as kids letting them stay downtown after nine o 'clock, that was out. Of course, that was out at that time anyhow, nine, ten o 'clock. Even if they went to a dance, they had to be home at eleven o 'clock.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But everybody was that way at that time. If they danced till twelve o 'clock, they'd shut the dance hall up and everybody went home. They'd go and eat lunch and then they'd go home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Between you and your wife, did you share the decisions about what would be done or did you make them?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, the wife. I let her handle that pretty much herself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How the house would be run, what the kids would do and that sort of thing?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would be the difference between the two of you?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Kids were a whole lot different then than they were now. They didn't have cars and stuff to tear around with like they do now.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: If they had a bicycle, they thought they had the world up with their tail. Now unless they got a car and a motorcycle and all kind of money to spend, they think they're badly abused.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who's doing that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I don't know, that chair seems to... Well, you've been sitting in it for quite a while, a couple of hours. Very long, it seems to crank my leg.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that you spent more of your time or paid more attention to your kin like your sister and brothers than you did with say just local people, friends, local friends?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Yes. So it's a close family.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: That's my polar bear.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: One thing with the old lumberjack, that way they didn't have very much moral standing with their selves. Now I've heard lots of them old lumberjacks saying that they, one especially, he had cancer. And he says, I'll tell you, he says cancer's not going to eat me up alive. He says, I'll take a gun and blow my head off. Well, there was a lot of them done that. They had cancer and they done that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say moral standing with themselves, you mean they didn't respect, they didn't have too much respect for themselves?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh, they had respect but they, you know, a Christian would never kill himself. And they, they was hard.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And they never had any fear. I was up in the hotel down here one time, went up to see one of the, I forget what his name is now.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: And he says, come in buyers, he says come in and see a man die like a man. Well, they were hard. He died. He died that, just about an hour after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From what?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, he was sick.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: But...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he just talk to you while you were there?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh yes. Yes. There's a lot on the porch there to get that over. Yes, that one's not water. But they never had no fear of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They weren't very religious most of them, were they?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: No, no, they wasn't religious at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that too?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Some of them were and some wasn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: We had lots of ... in through here at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think that was pretty enough?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Didn't believe in any creator or anything. Ah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, you yourself, did you become religious as you got older?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Oh no, I was born in a Christian home.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: I had... in Anderson, Indiana.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: That... has been a minister all of his life. Named me when I was born. Mother never...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She says, what are you going to call him?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Well, she says I haven't named him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, would you call him Byers?

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: His first name, named him after me and... so they did. They named me after him. I got writings in a book there that just came out the other day from Anderson, Indiana.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: Been in the gospel work all of his life.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: My mother was a very valid Christian. John was a very valid... Lila was. All of her family. All but one. Frank, my brother, next to me he was... He catered to it, but he was wild.

speaker: Byers Sanderson
words: He's a millionaire. No, they...

speaker: Byers Sanderson
